title: Your First Go Program
instruction: Welcome to Go! Every Go program has two required pieces: `package main` tells Go this is a runnable program, and `func main()` is where execution begins. The program below is already complete — just click **Run** to see it work.
hint: No changes needed — just press Run and see "Hello, World!" appear in the output.

title: Say Your Name
instruction: Great — Go is running! Now make it personal. Change the message so it prints **your** name. For example, if your name is Alex, print `Hello, Alex!`.
hint: Change the text inside Println to "Hello, Alex!" — replace Alex with your own name.

title: Print Two Lines
instruction:
Each call to `fmt.Println()` prints one line and then moves to the next line automatically. Print two lines: your name on the first line, and your hometown on the second line.

You're starting to build a student profile — we'll use this idea a lot throughout the course.
hint: Call fmt.Println() twice — once for your name, once for your hometown.

title: Fill in the Blanks with Printf
instruction:
`fmt.Printf()` lets you insert values into a template using **format verbs**. `%s` is a placeholder for a string, `%d` for a whole number, and `\n` moves to the next line.

The variables `name` and `year` are already set. Use `fmt.Printf` to print:
```
Welcome to Riverside Academy, 2025
```
hint: fmt.Printf("Welcome to %s, %d\n", name, year) — %s inserts the string, %d inserts the number.

title: Build a String with Sprintf
instruction:
`fmt.Sprintf()` works exactly like Printf but **returns a string** instead of printing it — perfect when you need to build a message and use it later.

Build the string `"Student ID: 1042"` using Sprintf with the variable `id`, store it in `badge`, then print it.
hint: badge := fmt.Sprintf("Student ID: %d", id) — then fmt.Println(badge).

title: Document Your Code with Comments
instruction:
Comments start with `//` and are completely ignored by Go — they exist only for humans reading the code. Good comments explain *why* something is done, not just *what*.

Add a comment above the Println explaining what the program is for, then make sure the program still prints `"Riverside Academy — Student Tracker"`.
hint: // This program is the entry point for Riverside Academy's student tracker

codeChecks.message: Add a // comment above the Println.
